cards.title: mazer: AI level, rank, and maze progression contract
cards.summary: Define how player level, player rank, AI level, AI rank, maze level, and maze complexity influence each other without making the loop feel arbitrary.
cards.whyItMatters: This is the backbone of the endless Mazer loop. If level, rank, complexity, and AI skill are vague, the game cannot scale fairly or feed useful learning receipts into Atlas.
cards.currentStatus: Level/rank still derive from target complexity at 72%, while the adjacent AI/playbook lane is at 97%; current proof covers target-derived bands, level-99 ceiling, difficulty profiles, fresh player seeds, score terms, compact AI decision receipts, rank-scaled AI perception profiles, human bias profiles, local human-lookahead scoring, non-chaotic AI exhaustion holds, and summary calibration that exposes whether AI score/signal movement actually changes level/rank.
workBreakdown: Reset AI progression defaults to level 1 unless account-backed saved value is intentionally loaded.
Define player level, AI level, rank, maze level, floor, and score in one player-facing and Atlas-facing contract.
Tie maze generation inputs to level bands without changing the physical screen size of the maze.
Score wrap paths as both shortcuts and topology complexity so easy teleports do not inflate difficulty incorrectly.
Keep rank S as top rank while target complexity and visible level keep moving toward level 99.
nextActions: Use the summary calibration output to tune why the default AI sample remains too ease-heavy.
Tune floor/rank scaling and progression pacing from real receipt bands.
Wire Atlas-side consumer ratchets from validated cycle-learning receipts.
acceptanceCriteria: Player performance can raise or lower next maze level through a documented numeric contract.
AI progression stays separate from player progression and can be reset/tuned independently.
Bleed-off edge paths are scored as traversal shortcuts and topology complexity.
Rank-S AI at target complexity 180 can keep progressing instead of capping at level 44.
proofPlan: Run focused Mazer progression and marker tests.
Run mobile UI surface capture and confirm level display still fits.
Inspect cycle receipts for player, AI, maze, score, route, and complexity fields.

cards.title: mazer: human-configured AI runner
cards.summary: Replace solver-like behavior with a human-style runner that perceives, classifies, commits, doubts, recovers, and learns without secretly routing through the full maze graph.
cards.whyItMatters: The AI is the game's tuning dummy. If it knows the end or receives a hidden full-floor route, score, rank, maze difficulty, and player-facing complexity all become misleading.
cards.currentStatus: The hidden full-floor emergency fallback is removed; A*/shortest-path data is a post-run benchmark; and the AI exposes thought, choice, confidence, remembered options, and a non-goal target. Rank and level drive local lookahead, retarget budget, uncertainty, wrap mental cost, and diagnostics-only solve preview. Rank sweeps show zero goal-target leaks, but finite local-route exhaustion still reaches reset/regeneration instead of expanding from a remembered unexplored frontier. That recovery replacement is the selected next slice.
workBreakdown: Use a noisy vision model: clear corridors are trusted more than dense intersections, wraps, and near-overlapping paths.
Separate perception from route choice by labeling branches as promising, risky, dead-end-looking, shortcut-looking, or unclear before selecting a move.
Keep A*/shortest path only as an internal benchmark for scoring after a run.
Add rank-based lookahead budgets from low-rank local junction checks to high-rank partial top-down planning.
Use implemented human bias profiles such as direct-chaser, wall-follower, shortcut-gambler, cautious mapper, and speedrunner.
Use confidence to decide whether to commit, doubt, retarget to a remembered split, or recover from a bad branch.
Apply mental cost to wrap paths so folded-world shortcuts become easier only as skill/rank improves.
Expose clean visible thought states: exploring, committing, doubting, recovering, shortcut-testing, and goal-confirming.
Replace any route-to-end fallback with known-frontier expansion.
Color active non-goal target tiles by current thought state while keeping remembered options visible as distinct memory markers.

Maintain npm run ai:calibrate, npm run ai:calibrate:ranks, and npm run ai:calibrate:biases as repeatable generated-seed proof for route ratio, reach/regeneration counts, invalid moves, goal-target leaks, thought states, choice classes, resolved perception profile, rank-sweep average-score comparison, route-efficiency-pressure comparison, and bias-profile separation.
Add richer UI visualization beyond the amber target tile, including branch labels, remembered options, target confidence, and current thought state.
nextActions: Replace finite-route exhaustion with persistent known-frontier expansion: persist seen splits, select the nearest verified unexplored frontier, and replan without injecting the unseen goal.
Prove pre-goal exhaustion cannot reset, regenerate, teleport, or target the end; expose recovering in diagnostics.
Add richer high-rank distinction beyond the current B-grade controller floor without reintroducing hidden solver behavior.
Keep default E-rank calibration near the healthier challenge/ease/hold mix while reducing regeneration count through route-quality improvements.
Run longer rank samples after each controller/generator tuning pass and compare goalTargetLeakPass, monotonicAverageScorePass, and monotonicRouteEfficiencyPass.
Add a compact player-facing legend for the thought-state colors and target-confidence visualization.
acceptanceCriteria: The AI cannot use a hidden full-floor route or reset/regenerate shortcut to reach the end after local memory fails.
The AI never exposes the end as its target before it has actually confirmed or reached it.
AI scoring compares against shortest viable path after the run without using that route to control movement.
The maintained calibration command reports zero goal-target leaks and zero invalid moves across the default generated-seed sample.
Visible thought overlays explain why the AI is exploring, committing, doubting, recovering, or testing a shortcut.
Runtime diagnostics expose thought state, choice class, confidence, option points, and non-goal target point for screenshot QA.
Low-rank AI remains fallible while high-rank AI can plan larger chunks without becoming a perfect solver.
proofPlan: Run focused demo-walker tests for no hidden goal target and no full-floor fallback.
Run npm run ai:calibrate -- --summary-only and inspect route ratio, leak, reach, regeneration, score, signal, level, rank, thought-state, and choice-class summaries.
Run generated multi-seed AI samples and inspect score/progression movement.
Capture menu AI screenshots showing remembered options, non-goal targets, and thought-state visualization.

cards.title: mazer: player-facing options and info guide
cards.summary: Turn the Options menu into a clean player guide that explains controls, compass, toggles, level/rank, and current progression without exposing internal diagnostics.
cards.whyItMatters: Players should understand the game without seeing internal debug language. The Options screen is the correct place for concise, branded explanations.
cards.currentStatus: Options and play pause now start with a centered Player Guide for Compass, Start, End, Player, Skill Lvl, Score, Runs, and Maze Lvl. The guide reuses the same compass/start/end renderers as the board, replaces the redundant player picture with clear bullets, hides developer-only scale controls, and has DPR-2 authenticated Options proof.
workBreakdown: Move the guide to the top of main-menu Options.
Reuse the guide inside played-game Pause before controls.
Remove the extra Game Toggles heading while preserving the controls.
Explain public badge fields without debug shorthand or internal complexity diagnostics.
Give mobile Options the same scroll viewport and rail affordance as Pause.
nextActions: Keep literal board-renderer glyphs in the guide when those assets evolve; do not duplicate their drawing contracts.
Run broader authenticated mobile Options and Pause soak.
Keep target-complexity and raw diagnostic fields out of normal player Options.
acceptanceCriteria: The player guide explains every visible badge field without clipping.
Normal Options and played-game Pause both expose a clear scroll affordance on phone layouts.
Developer-only scale and complexity diagnostics stay hidden from normal player Options.
proofPlan: Open Options on menu and play screens at desktop and mobile sizes.
Screenshot the exact Options surface before claiming visual completion.

cards.title: mazer: edge-wrap topology, border fill, and notch safety
cards.summary: Make wrapped border paths a real graph contract, keep start/goal/navigation aware of wrap edges, and prevent maze path visuals from entering border cutouts.
cards.whyItMatters: Wrap paths and border notches change both visuals and navigation. They must be graph-owned, not just visual tricks, or AI distance, player movement, and difficulty scoring drift apart.
cards.currentStatus: Wrap navigation exists and can snap visually, but generation, fill near triangular border cutouts, and difficulty scoring still need contract-level hardening.
workBreakdown: Guarantee at least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate only when legal for the current maze bounds.
Represent wrap neighbors in the graph so AI, player movement, compass, trail, and complexity formulas all agree.
Keep generated paths out of title/compass notches and folded-corner voids.
Improve bottom and corner fill so paths reach usable border edges without clipping into decorative cutouts.
nextActions: Add topology diagnostics for wrap-pair validity.
Add notch-safe render masks or generator constraints.
Capture visual proof for top notch, bottom notch, and all four folded corners.
acceptanceCriteria: At least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate can be generated when complexity allows it.
AI distance and route choice account for wrap neighbors instead of treating them as visual-only paths.
Path rendering respects the folded border notches and fills toward edges without leaking into cutout voids.
proofPlan: Run topology diagnostics against generated mazes with wrap paths enabled.
Capture a visual proof where paths reach the border edges but do not enter the top notch.

cards.title: mazer: crisp cyber-arcade graphics and art consistency
cards.summary: Raise the full game visual system toward a crisp 2026 cyber-arcade target: sharper tiles, cleaner borders, less blur, stronger material consistency.
cards.whyItMatters: The game feel depends heavily on the maze being readable and premium on mobile. Blurry tiles or inconsistent materials make the app feel older than the current design target.
cards.currentStatus: HiDPI backing and several green/cyan material passes have landed, but the visual language still needs one consistent high-tech arcade material system across all surfaces.
workBreakdown: Remove accidental blur/fuzz from canvas scaling, tile seams, title pieces, and UI text.
Unify path, trail, pulse, player, title, border, buttons, compass, and background under one material palette.
Use actual mobile screenshots as proof, not desktop-only visual checks.
Track the approved icon image as the current visual quality target.
nextActions: Audit every draw layer for fractional pixels and scaling mismatches.
Create a visual comparison sheet for icon target versus current menu/play screen.
Add screenshot proof gates for 390-430px mobile widths.
acceptanceCriteria: Canvas backing scale and camera scale stay crisp at mobile and desktop sizes.
Path, trail, title, border, buttons, compass, and background share a coherent material language.
Visual QA includes screenshots from the actual target screen, not vague route captures.
proofPlan: Run desktop and mobile screenshot checks with route/version labels visible.
Compare against the approved Mazer icon image as the art target.

cards.title: mazer: icon-quality 2026 visual target
cards.summary: Use the new app icon as the north-star for sharper glow, material depth, palette, and shape language across the game.
cards.whyItMatters: The icon now communicates the target style better than the in-game visuals. The game should inherit that crisp cyan/green glow, deep glass, and arcade geometry instead of drifting into muddy retro blur.
cards.currentStatus: The icon has been provided as a reference, but the game has not fully absorbed its material quality, contrast, or depth.
workBreakdown: Save canonical app icon assets for favicon, browser, install surfaces, and future Atlas/link/Stripe references.
Extract the icon palette into a reusable Mazer visual target note.
Translate icon material rules into game draw-layer rules.
Use the icon as the art QA baseline for future screenshots.
nextActions: Verify the current icon assets are wired everywhere expected.
Add Atlas-facing visual reference documentation.
Start material parity work for title, border, path, player, trail, and buttons.
acceptanceCriteria: App icon assets are saved and wired for browser, Vercel, and app surfaces.
Game materials adopt the icon's crisp cyan/green glow, dark glass, and high-contrast arcade geometry.
Atlas keeps the icon reference available for later Stripe/link/auth surfaces.
proofPlan: Verify favicon and app icon assets resolve locally.
Capture menu and play screenshots showing icon-inspired palette and crispness improvements.

cards.title: mazer: fluid controls, player motion, and path feel
cards.summary: Replace snappy tile jumps and hard-coded stick segments with smoother player motion and dynamic directional intent handling.
cards.whyItMatters: Fast timed play will fail if the controls feel segmented, sticky, or jumpy. The player should feel like they are steering through a path, not stepping through a debug grid.
cards.currentStatus: Tap/hold parity and wrap snap improvements exist, but the stick still needs a true intent resolver and player motion still needs smoother interpolation.
workBreakdown: Make stick angle represent movement intent rather than hard-coded segment commands.
Resolve intent against legal graph neighbors dynamically every movement step.
Smooth visual player motion while preserving exact maze-cell legality.
Keep single-click, hold, keyboard, on-screen arrows, and stick behavior consistent.
nextActions: Design the dynamic intent resolver before changing input visuals again.
Add QA scripts for tap, hold, diagonal-ish intent, blocked wall, release, and wrap snap cases.
Tune movement speed with pace/level formulas instead of one fixed feel.
acceptanceCriteria: Single presses move exactly once while holds continue only while active.
Stick direction is interpreted as intent, then resolved against legal path neighbors dynamically.
Wrap transitions snap cleanly instead of dragging the player across the whole screen.
proofPlan: Run live play QA for quick taps, holds, stick drags, and wrap paths.
Confirm no phantom movement remains after release.

cards.title: mazer: play camera zoom slider and minimap
cards.summary: Add a played-game-only camera zoom slider under the maze and a bottom-left minimap overlay after zoom is enabled.
cards.whyItMatters: Player-centered zoom can make dense mazes feel bigger and easier to read without scaling the whole app. A minimap preserves full-maze awareness when the camera is zoomed in.
cards.currentStatus: Deferred later-stage feature. The marker exists, but implementation should wait until play lifecycle, mobile layout, smooth movement, and camera diagnostics are stable.
workBreakdown: Add a slider bar under the played-game maze only.
Zoom only the in-border maze camera toward the player; do not change browser/app zoom or UI scale.
Keep the camera smoothly locked above the player as the player moves.
Preserve smooth player interpolation while the camera follows.
After zoom is enabled, add a bottom-left minimap about one-eighth the maze-border size.
Render the minimap as a full unzoomed maze overview with player, start, end, trail/route state, and relevant markers.
nextActions: Define the shared viewport contract for maze camera, minimap, compass, trail, and diagnostics.
Design slider bounds and default zoom without disrupting mobile HUD spacing.
Prototype camera-follow math behind diagnostics before shipping UI.
Add visual proof for unzoomed, mid-zoom, max-zoom, and minimap states.
acceptanceCriteria: The slider changes only the maze camera inside the border.
The player remains smoothly centered during movement at every zoom level.
The minimap shows the full maze and overlays inside the bottom-left of the maze without blocking controls.
Start, end, player, trail, and important markers are visible on the minimap.
Mobile and desktop screenshots prove no HUD or maze-border overlap.
proofPlan: Run route-aware play-mode captures at default, mid, and max zoom.
Drive the player through several moves and verify camera-follow diagnostics stay smooth.
Capture minimap proof showing player, start, end, and full maze bounds.

cards.title: mazer: auth, remote receipts, Atlas, and playbook sync
cards.summary: Finish the account-backed progression loop, remote cycle receipts, and Atlas/playbook handoff without exposing internal debug surfaces to players.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer needs continuity. Account-backed progress, cycle receipts, and Atlas reports are what let the game learn, tune difficulty, and preserve user/AI history across sessions.
cards.currentStatus: Remote cycle receipts, player progression, AI progression, Supabase read/write proof, Atlas-safe reports, compact AI decision scores, shortest-path comparison fields, render-safety pressure, and bounded AI reset proof are in place; Atlas-side consumer wiring remains open.
workBreakdown: Keep compact remote cycle receipts behind the opt-in env flag and authenticated guard.
Preserve player and AI progression tracks independently.
Normalize direct Supabase row exports into Atlas-safe cycle-learning reports.
Carry AI decision score, route-efficiency pressure, shortest-path overrun, and render-safety pressure in compact receipts.
Bound account AI reset work to the AI progression table only.
nextActions: Wire the validator-gated consumer receipt into an Atlas-side process.
Calibrate report trends from longer player and AI sample bands.
Keep remote sync player-safe and non-noisy for guest, missing-client, disabled, and failure outcomes.
acceptanceCriteria: Remote receipts and progression states remain account-scoped and table-bounded.
Atlas reports omit raw path history while preserving tuning context.
A real Atlas-side consumer can read the report and produce bounded next-work guidance without silent auto-tuning.
proofPlan: Run auth UI QA with intentional failure and success states.
Verify Supabase rows for progression and cycle receipt readback through browser-safe RLS.

cards.title: mazer: unified player-facing message system
cards.summary: Move auth and gameplay notices into the same clean player-facing message system instead of leaving raw status text on screen.
cards.whyItMatters: Raw technical messages break the game feel. Errors, auth notices, and offline states should appear as intentional in-game messages, not development leftovers.
cards.currentStatus: The need is identified and some auth messages have been touched, but every error/status path still needs to route through one consistent message surface.
workBreakdown: Route login/create/fetch/auth failures through the shared message system.
Remove raw setup and network text from visible game menus.
Standardize severity, duration, placement, and animation.
Make the message style match the existing Fawxzzy app notification pattern while staying Mazer-themed.
nextActions: Inventory every visible status/error string in menu, auth, Options, and play Pause.
Replace inline status labels with message events.
Add visual QA for failed login, failed create, fetch failure, success, and logout.
acceptanceCriteria: Login/create/fetch/auth failures route through the player-facing message surface.
Messages use consistent style, duration, severity, and placement across menu and play screens.
No raw 'failed to fetch' or setup strings remain embedded in the login menu.
proofPlan: Trigger failed login, failed create, successful login, logout, and offline/fetch failure.
Screenshot each state and verify no text overlap.

cards.title: mazer: auth gate and persistent login parity
cards.summary: Finish the player-facing account gate and persistent-login behavior so Mazer matches the useful Fitness app login continuity without exposing auth internals.
cards.whyItMatters: The game needs account continuity for progression, AI-runner history, and remote receipts, but the login flow still has to feel like a game surface instead of a debug form.
cards.currentStatus: Native inputs, remembered identity, local-only sign-out, real account proof, and production deploy alias proof exist. Remaining risk is longer mobile/form soak plus deeper production auth parity checks.
workBreakdown: Keep guests on the animated menu with Login as the only gated action.
Preserve signed-in sessions across reloads without storing tokens in local app state.
Route auth errors through the unified player message system.
Verify local and production Supabase env parity.
nextActions: Run a mobile auth soak for login, reload persistence, logout, and re-login.
Verify production env uses only browser-safe Supabase keys.
Keep account logout available only from main-menu account surfaces.
acceptanceCriteria: A returning signed-in user lands on the normal main menu without re-entering credentials.
A signed-out or guest user sees the animated menu with Login only.
No raw auth/network text appears in the login surface.
proofPlan: Run visible browser QA for failed login, successful login, reload persistence, and logout.
Read diagnostics only for auth state and never expose hidden technical detail to users.

cards.title: mazer: animation smoothness and lifecycle cadence
cards.summary: Remove visible pauses, loop resets, and buffered animation stalls across title orbit, scanner, background, border glimmer, trail pulse, build, and deconstruct.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer depends on continuous motion. Any hitch during build/deconstruct or title/background loops makes the whole app feel laggy even when gameplay logic is correct.
cards.currentStatus: Several frame cadence fixes landed, but mobile soak and lifecycle-boundary jitter review remain open.
workBreakdown: Keep title, compass, background, and border animations alive during deconstruct pauses.
Make scanner/orbit loops bounce or wrap without visible reset jumps.
Profile mobile frame cadence during build, deconstruct, and pause overlays.
Record animation proof with actual route and viewport contracts.
nextActions: Run a long mobile menu loop soak.
Capture lifecycle handoff frames around goal, deconstruct, empty pause, and rebuild.
Patch any remaining timer or tween coupling that pauses global animations.
acceptanceCriteria: No visible title, compass, or background freeze occurs during maze handoff.
Build and deconstruct keep crisp staged motion on mobile and desktop.
Runtime diagnostics show stable frame timing during lifecycle transitions.
proofPlan: Record route-aware screenshots or video frames through a full menu and play lifecycle.
Compare frame timing before and after animation handoff changes.

cards.title: mazer: play-mode perpetual completion loop
cards.summary: Keep players locked into play after reaching the end by recording the cycle, deconstructing cleanly, rebuilding a fresh maze, then restarting play without returning to menu.
cards.whyItMatters: The core game loop should be endless and progression-backed. Completion should feel like a polished arcade handoff, not a reset or dead stop.
cards.currentStatus: First lifecycle pass works in production proof: QA can drive to goal, observe goal-hold, deconstruct, handoff, fresh build, compass spin, and ready state. Title parity, timing polish, and longer held-control soak remain.
workBreakdown: Record completion and shortest-path comparison before the next maze starts.
Lock input during deconstruct/build while keeping compass and ambient motion active.
Generate each next player maze from current skill, score, and target complexity.
Keep Reset/Menu in pause as explicit player choices.
nextActions: Polish deconstruct timing after player goal contact.
Decide whether play mode gets the Mazer title above the maze.
Run longer held-stick tests across lifecycle boundaries.
acceptanceCriteria: After reaching the end, play mode deconstructs and rebuilds a fresh procedural maze.
The timer excludes build time and restarts only when the new maze is playable.
Player progress is saved before returning to menu or closing the app.
proofPlan: Drive a real play maze to the goal with runtime QA.
Verify post-goal lifecycle pass, fresh seed, locked input during build, and timer reset.

cards.title: mazer: iridescent player, trail, and pulse material
cards.summary: Design and tune a midnight-rainbow material for player halo, trail, and pulse while keeping the core player readable and separated from path color.
cards.whyItMatters: The player and trail need to communicate progression and AI thinking without blending into pale path tiles or becoming visually noisy.
cards.currentStatus: Runtime material helpers and tests exist. Screenshots, saturation tuning, and dense-maze visual QA remain.
workBreakdown: Keep the player core locked to a high-contrast green readability anchor.
Shift halo, accent, trail, and pulse through a controlled midnight-rainbow palette.
Prevent trail/player colors from closely matching maze path colors.
Use AI memory/target overlays without confusing them with the active trail.
nextActions: Capture dense-maze screenshots with player, trail, memory, and target overlays visible.
Tune alpha and saturation for mobile readability.
Document material rules for future progression color tiers.
acceptanceCriteria: Player and trail stay visible against every path material tier.
Trail, pulse, memory, and target colors remain semantically distinct.
Visual diagnostics expose enough material samples for regression checks.
proofPlan: Run material unit tests and visual smoke on dense menu/play mazes.
Compare screenshots at mobile and desktop widths.

cards.title: mazer: diagonal path graph contract
cards.markerName: Diagonal paths and true diagonal travel
cards.summary: Design diagonal tiles and true diagonal travel as a real graph feature before any visual-only diagonal paths are shipped.
cards.whyItMatters: Diagonal routes can unlock spirals and richer maze shapes, but only if generation, movement, AI distance, compass, trail, and telemetry all agree.
cards.currentStatus: The concept is intentionally deferred. Current diagonal controls are directional intent helpers over a cardinal graph, not true diagonal topology.
workBreakdown: Define diagonal neighbor legality and collision rules.
Design diagonal render joins that fill corner-touching gaps cleanly.
Update AI distance, shortest path, compass, trail, and receipts for diagonal edges.
Resolve diagonal interaction with edge-wrap paths.
nextActions: Write a graph contract spike before UI implementation.
Add generator and solver tests for diagonal neighbors.
Only then prototype diagonal tile art.
acceptanceCriteria: Diagonal paths are represented in the same graph used by player movement and AI.
Shortest-path and complexity metrics count diagonal edges correctly.
Diagonal visuals do not imply illegal movement.
proofPlan: Run topology tests for diagonal movement legality.
Capture visual proof only after graph tests pass.

cards.title: mazer: route-aware visual proof discipline
cards.summary: Require screenshots and visual claims to prove they are on the intended route, mode, overlay, and viewport before being accepted.
cards.whyItMatters: Several visual passes were weakened by vague screenshots. Mazer needs route-aware proof so fixes are validated on the exact surface the user asked about.
cards.currentStatus: Screen contracts exist across capture scripts, and live-play QA now models opposite-border wrapped steps instead of using a stale flat-grid route solver. Authenticated surface fixture proof and adoption discipline remain open.
workBreakdown: Fail captures when route, mode, overlay, or viewport contract does not match.
Record actual URL, diagnostics mode, surface, and viewport beside screenshots.
Use proof artifacts before claiming visual completion.
Add authenticated surface fixtures where needed.
nextActions: Extend proof workflow to any remaining auth-only surfaces.
Require visual closeouts to cite screenContract.pass.
Keep screenshots tied to the exact requested surface.
acceptanceCriteria: Visual proof includes actual route, mode, overlay, viewport, and diagnostics contract.
Wrong-screen screenshots fail the capture step.
Future DiscordOS card updates cite proof paths or blockers.
proofPlan: Run capture matrix and edge-live screenshot workflows.
Verify generated metadata includes screenContract.pass for each proof.

cards.title: mazer: DiscordOS board discipline and completion movement
cards.summary: Keep exactly one live Mazer board in Project Feedback Boards, keep cards richly formatted, and update card state/reactions as implementation work lands.
cards.whyItMatters: The board is only useful if it mirrors real work. Thin cards, wrong placement, missing reactions, or stale completion state break the planning loop.
cards.currentStatus: The live mazer forum exists in Project Feedback Boards with rich card readback. Visibility drift usually happens when a chat updates local board JSON or Mazer marker docs without running the guarded DiscordOS live sync and readback, or leaves DiscordOS changes uncommitted for the next chat.
workBreakdown: Use one forum named mazer under Project Feedback Boards.
Keep each card formatted with purpose, current state, work breakdown, next actions, acceptance criteria, and proof plan.
Apply failure reaction for not-done cards and success reaction for completed cards.
Move completed work to the completed workflow surface only after proof-backed closure.
nextActions: After any board JSON change, run env readiness, guarded live sync, live readback, and reconciliation in DiscordOS.
Commit DiscordOS board changes after successful live readback so other chats see the same state.
Keep Mazer marker changes and DiscordOS board cards reconciled before claiming card movement.
acceptanceCriteria: The live board name is mazer and is placed under Project Feedback Boards.
Every active Mazer work lane has a richly formatted card.
Completed cards receive success reaction/state only after proof.
proofPlan: Run live sync and live readback against the mazer forum.
Read back channel inventory proving the forum parent is Project Feedback Boards.

cards.title: mazer: account-scoped settings persistence
cards.summary: Make changed game settings become the authenticated account's saved defaults instead of one global browser preference.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer now has real accounts. If control mode, pause-facing settings, visual toggles, or movement speed leak between accounts, auth-backed progression will feel unreliable.
cards.currentStatus: The repo now scopes local game-toggle storage through the existing auth-scoped storage helper, reloads settings on account changes, and has guest/user isolation tests. Browser auth-flow proof and legacy unscoped preference migration remain open.
workBreakdown: Extend the existing preference path instead of creating a second persistence system. Done locally.
Store settings under the authenticated account when a session exists. Done locally.
Load settings when the account/session initializes and account changes. Done locally.
Prevent account switching and logout from reusing another account's preferences. Covered by storage isolation tests.
Cover controller, pause-facing, movement, background, camera, trail, and theme toggles.
nextActions: Run browser auth-flow proof for login/logout/reload settings persistence.
Decide whether to migrate older unscoped local preferences into guest scope or leave them behind.
Verify visible Options and play pause toggles load the authenticated account defaults.
acceptanceCriteria: Settings are stored and loaded under the authenticated account.
One account's preferences cannot leak into another account.
Missing or corrupt saved preferences fall back to app defaults without corrupting settings.
Save/load failures are handled without breaking gameplay.
proofPlan: Run the game-toggle preference unit tests.
Run an auth-switching preference test using two account-scoped storage keys.
Run a browser Options smoke proving reload persistence for the logged-in account.

cards.title: mazer: player input and movement correctness
cards.summary: Fix the movement correctness defects where diagonal movement can be faster than cardinal movement and one arrow-key action can move the player twice.
cards.whyItMatters: Skill, score, route efficiency, and maze difficulty all depend on movement being fair and measurable. Input bugs poison progression analytics.
cards.currentStatus: Shared input plumbing, repeat gating, pause locks, and diagonal legality tests exist. QA bridge moves, pointer swipe releases, and buffered keyboard held diagonals now route through the same one-visible-step diagonal planner as touch controls; full browser/mobile proof remains open.
workBreakdown: Audit keydown, keyup, browser repeat, and duplicate listener paths. First pass complete for buffered keyboard path.
Ensure one accepted arrow-key command produces one visible movement step. Source-level guard added; browser proof still needed.
Normalize diagonal travel so it has no speed or distance advantage over cardinal travel. Common scene input entry points now use the cardinal-step planner.
Keep keyboard and controller routed through one canonical movement-command path where practical.
Preserve pause/input-lock behavior.
nextActions: Run live browser/mobile proof for one accepted input producing one visible movement step.
Soak keyboard repeat and stick repeat rates against diagnostics.
Keep true diagonal pathing deferred until generation, graph, trail, compass, and AI distance share one contract.
acceptanceCriteria: One accepted arrow-key input advances exactly one step.
Duplicate or invalid input events cannot advance the player twice.
Diagonal movement is not faster than up, down, left, or right movement.
Keyboard and controller behavior stay aligned through shared movement plumbing.
proofPlan: Run input-human keyboard and touch tests.
Run legacy play-step tests for cardinal, diagonal, wall, and pause cases.
Run a live play smoke that samples diagnostics before and after one input event.

cards.title: mazer: procedural difficulty generator shaping
cards.summary: Turn player and AI skill into generated maze structure instead of only badge text.
cards.whyItMatters: The endless progression loop needs mazes that actually match the active target complexity, not just UI labels that say the skill changed.
cards.currentStatus: Difficulty profiles now map target complexity into scale, checkpoint pressure, shortcut pressure, route-quality reinforcement, required opposite-border wraps, and perimeter feeder pressure; generation requests carry target complexity and bounded seed selection chooses the closest candidate, but deeper retry/search and long-run calibration remain open.
workBreakdown: Define tutorial, starter, explorer, navigator, architect, and mythic difficulty profiles.
Map the active player or AI track into executable generation pressure terms.
Expose generationReview with under-target, on-target, and over-target classification.
Select from a bounded seed window instead of blindly accepting the first generated maze.
nextActions: Add deeper generator retry/search when the whole candidate window misses target.
Tune branch, fill, dead-end, and wrap-path pressure against longer samples.
Prove the endless progression loop can rise and fall smoothly across player and AI runs.
acceptanceCriteria: Generated maze complexity tracks the active player or AI target band within a bounded search cost.
Diagnostics explain whether each maze under-delivered, matched, or overshot the target.
Long-run proof shows difficulty changes remain readable and stable instead of jumping or stalling.
proofPlan: Run progression and generation tests for difficulty profile selection.
Run cycle telemetry reports across representative player and AI sample bands.
Read back generationReview diagnostics for low, mid, and high target complexity runs.

cards.title: mazer: mobile shell, portrait lock, and device harness
cards.summary: Decide and prove the phone-shell path for mobile view, portrait behavior, and real-device confidence.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer is playable in browser emulation, but hard portrait guarantees and real-device trust need an explicit shell/device harness decision.
cards.currentStatus: The app remains a web/PWA runtime with local mobile emulation proof; 405x958 captures cover guest menu, auth, play, pause, mobile Options, scroll rails, and phone HUD spacing, but true hard portrait lock requires a native/PWA install context or wrapper decision.
workBreakdown: Keep phone-size UI-surface proof at 405x958 for guest, auth, play, and pause surfaces.
Assert active labels fit and progression badge text stays inside its chrome.
Render visible scroll rails for mobile Options and played-game Pause.
Reserve the phone play top lane for centered skill badge and far-right pause action.
nextActions: Run broader authenticated mobile soak on a real or maintained device harness.
Choose best-effort web orientation handling versus Capacitor/Tauri/native wrapper support.
Prove play controls, pause scroll, auth native inputs, and longer animation cycles under DPR-2.
acceptanceCriteria: Phone portrait layouts keep text, HUD, controls, and maze bounds non-overlapping.
Orientation behavior is honestly documented as web best-effort or shell-enforced.
A repeatable device harness can prove the mobile app without relying only on desktop emulation.
proofPlan: Run UI surface capture at 405x958.
Run authenticated Options and played-game Pause screenshots with scroll rail diagnostics.
Run high-DPI mobile smoke for play controls, pause, and auth fields.

cards.title: mazer: visual defaults and menu controls
cards.summary: Lock the fresh-session defaults, menu control placement, and player-safe menu/play control language.
cards.whyItMatters: A stable default experience keeps new sessions readable and avoids leaking diagnostics or confusing settings into the first-play flow.
cards.currentStatus: Fresh sessions default to dark mode, animated background, stick controls, trail fade off, trail pulse on, camera follow off, 30% move speed, green action/player/trail/level palette, guest Login-only, a notch compass, and scroll-rail Options/Pause. Main-menu Options omits Move Speed; played-game Pause retains it, adds on/off toggle descriptions, and includes confirmed Reset Progress.
workBreakdown: Keep the canonical fresh-session default contract aligned with current runtime behavior.
Keep guest entry Login-only and authenticated Start/Options spacing clean.
Keep Account/Log out on main-menu account surfaces, not played-game Pause.
Use Reset for current-maze restart, Reset Progress for a confirmed player-progression-only reset, and Menu for explicit opt-out from the perpetual loop.
Keep the visible play timer owned by the skill badge instead of a duplicate timer panel.
nextActions: Run longer mobile menu soak across guest, authenticated, Options, play, and pause states.
Keep Start, player, trail, and level colors aligned to the green action language.
Recheck defaults whenever new settings or controls are added.
acceptanceCriteria: Fresh sessions resolve to the documented defaults without stale browser preference leakage.
Menu and play controls are player-safe and do not expose internal diagnostics.
Mobile and desktop layouts show Start/Options, badge, pause, and maze with clear spacing.
proofPlan: Run menu layout and render-frame tests.
Capture guest, authenticated, Options, play, and pause surfaces at phone and desktop sizes.
Read diagnostics for text fit, scroll affordance, and no-overlap state.

cards.title: mazer: AI token assisted maze completion
cards.summary: Let an account earn AI Tokens and spend one during a playable maze so the existing AI player takes over and completes the remaining route.
cards.whyItMatters: This gives players a bounded assist mechanic while reusing the AI navigation system as visible, account-aware gameplay instead of only menu telemetry.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The existing player AI/navigation implementation is the dependency; no active implementation is started in this pass.
workBreakdown: Award one AI Token for every 10 completed levels and store tokens per account.
Allow token consumption during an active playable maze level.
Disable manual controls while the existing AI player controls the runner.
Apply selected player customization to the AI-controlled player.
Integrate the first-token moment with the reusable tutorial system.
nextActions: Resolve token cap, stacking, failed-level behavior, and exact consumption timing.
Define how token use affects scoring and progression receipts.
Design the player-facing token control and tutorial entry point.
acceptanceCriteria: Tokens are earned and stored per account.
A consumed token hands control to the existing AI runner until the maze completes.
Manual input is disabled while AI assist is active.
Unresolved token economy decisions remain documented instead of guessed.
proofPlan: Run account persistence tests for token balance.
Run play-mode handoff proof from manual to AI-controlled navigation.
Run visual proof that customization persists during AI control.

cards.title: mazer: reusable first-time tutorial and feature-introduction system
cards.summary: Create a reusable account-scoped tutorial registry for onboarding and first-occurrence feature explanations.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer is accumulating mechanics that need clear introduction without hardcoding a one-off popup for each new feature.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The first AI Token is the initial use case, but the system should be reusable and data driven.
workBreakdown: Trigger first-time informational screens per account.
Support new-account onboarding and first-occurrence events.
Show a representative image or asset, explanation text, and a Continue action.
Persist tutorial acknowledgement state separately per account.
Include accessibility, responsive layout, and input-focus requirements.
nextActions: Define the tutorial registry data shape.
Choose where account acknowledgement state should live.
Draft the first-token tutorial content and visual asset contract.
acceptanceCriteria: Tutorials are registered through data, not one-off UI branches.
Acknowledgement state is account-scoped and survives reload.
Account switching preserves separate tutorial histories.
The UI works on mobile and keyboard-focused browser input.
proofPlan: Run account switching tests for tutorial acknowledgement isolation.
Run mobile visual proof for tutorial layout.
Run keyboard focus proof for Continue navigation.

cards.title: mazer: customization inventory and cosmetic unlocks
cards.summary: Add the account customization inventory and unlock surface, replacing the need for a separate standalone shop card.
cards.whyItMatters: Cosmetics need one clear account-owned surface that can handle owned, equipped, locked, direct unlock, random unlock, and future catalog expansion.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. No separate shop card should be created for this scope.
workBreakdown: Add a smaller square clothing-hanger button left of Start on the main menu.
Organize customization options by category with default first options.
Show owned, equipped, locked, checkmark, and random-selection states.
Support direct unlock and random unlock from the selected category.
Persist owned and selected cosmetics per account and apply them to manual and AI-controlled players.
nextActions: Resolve the direct-unlock currency or payment model.
Design the inventory layout and reveal animation.
Connect inventory state to the data-driven cosmetic catalog.
acceptanceCriteria: The inventory surface replaces separate shop scope.
Owned and selected cosmetics are account-scoped.
Manual and AI-controlled player rendering use the selected visuals.
Direct and random unlock paths are represented without assuming real-money purchase.
proofPlan: Run account persistence tests for owned and equipped cosmetics.
Run menu visual proof for hanger button sizing and placement.
Run inventory interaction proof for equip and random unlock reveal.

cards.title: mazer: maze coin pickups and cosmetic currency
cards.summary: Add animated coin pickups to valid maze tiles and persist a cosmetic-only account currency balance.
cards.whyItMatters: Cosmetic unlocks need an in-game economy that does not corrupt maze scoring, AI behavior, or progression difficulty metrics.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Currency is intended only for customization and cosmetic unlocks.
workBreakdown: Place animated rotating coins on accessible valid maze tiles.
Collect coins when the player enters a coin tile.
Persist account coin balance.
Scale coin availability with maze level, maze size, difficulty, and progression.
Keep coin collection out of maze generation metrics, scoring, progression scaling, and AI behavior.
nextActions: Define seeded placement and duplicate-collection safeguards.
Design balance display and pickup animation.
Set rules for avoiding inaccessible placements and start/end collisions.
acceptanceCriteria: Coins are accessible and deterministic where replay/testing requires it.
Balance persists per account.
Coin collection does not affect difficulty, score, or AI pathing.
Visual and performance cost stay bounded.
proofPlan: Run seeded placement tests.
Run account balance persistence tests.
Run play-mode visual proof for pickup and balance display.

cards.title: mazer: data-driven cosmetic and theme catalog
cards.summary: Define cosmetic and theme options through data and reusable asset references instead of hardcoded UI branches.
cards.whyItMatters: The inventory system can only scale if ownership, selection, rendering, and unlock cost stay separated from each cosmetic's presentation.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Possible categories are recorded as future options, not committed implementation scope.
workBreakdown: Support possible player, trail, pulse, maze, border, path, background, title, theme, coin, sound, and screen-element categories.
Represent cosmetics through reusable data and asset references.
Separate ownership, selection, rendering, and unlock-cost concerns.
Avoid one-off conditionals for every cosmetic.
Support future catalog expansion without rewriting the inventory UI.
nextActions: Draft the catalog data model.
Map current visual surfaces to candidate cosmetic slots.
Link asset registration to the handcrafted asset workflow.
acceptanceCriteria: Cosmetics are data driven and reusable.
Inventory can render categories without per-category branching.
Ownership and selected-state logic stay separate from rendering.
New cosmetic categories can be added without rewriting the core UI.
proofPlan: Run catalog schema tests.
Run inventory render tests against sample catalog fixtures.
Run visual proof for at least one catalog-backed cosmetic category.

cards.title: mazer: handcrafted asset authoring and import workflow
cards.summary: Document and validate a path for importing personally created materials, icons, images, animations, and other assets.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer needs a repeatable asset pipeline so future high-quality visuals do not depend on ad hoc generated files or unclear licensing.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. This is tooling and documentation infrastructure, not a requirement to create all final assets now.
workBreakdown: Define accepted file formats, dimensions, names, directories, metadata, optimization, and previews.
Validate invalid, oversized, or missing assets.
Record source and licensing metadata where appropriate.
Make asset registration compatible with the cosmetic catalog.
Reduce dependence on automatically generated visuals.
nextActions: Choose canonical asset directories and metadata fields.
Draft validation rules for imported art and animation assets.
Connect validated assets to catalog references.
acceptanceCriteria: A documented import path exists for handcrafted assets.
Validation catches missing, oversized, or malformed assets.
Asset metadata supports source and license tracking.
Imported assets can be referenced by the cosmetic catalog.
proofPlan: Run asset fixture validation tests.
Run a sample catalog registration using a local asset fixture.
Run visual proof for one imported asset reference.

cards.title: mazer: enemy pursuit AI
cards.summary: Add deterministic enemy navigation that pursues the player's current position instead of the maze endpoint.
cards.whyItMatters: Enemies are a major difficulty and urgency layer, but they need a testable movement contract before combat or item systems are added.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Enemy spawn rules, counts, difficulty scaling, visual design, balancing, and item interactions remain unresolved; future pursuit must respect cloak masking, frozen-enemy duration windows, and multiplayer target ownership.
workBreakdown: Reuse existing player-AI compass/navigation concepts where they fit.
Use pathfinding toward the visible, targetable player position rather than the maze endpoint.
Recalculate or invalidate route as the player moves.
Respect item effects such as cloak masking, frozen enemy movement, and temporary pass-through collision.
Match enemy cadence with player movement unless future balance overrides it.
End the game on enemy collision or contact when no pass-through effect is active.
nextActions: Define spawn and count rules.
Decide how enemy difficulty scales by maze level and player rank.
Link enemy updates to the turn-synchronous simulation contract and cloak duration rules.
Define enemy target ownership for multiplayer sessions.
acceptanceCriteria: Enemy pathfinding is deterministic and testable.
Enemy targets the player, not the endpoint.
Route recalculation responds to player movement.
Collision outcome is explicit and covered by tests.
proofPlan: Run A* pursuit unit tests on static mazes.
Run turn simulation tests proving enemy movement order.
Run play-mode collision proof.

cards.title: mazer: turn-synchronous world simulation
cards.summary: Move gameplay-affecting entities onto a centralized turn command so the world advances only when accepted player movement advances the turn.
cards.whyItMatters: Enemies, projectiles, pickups, and collisions need predictable ordering and should not run ahead while the player is stopped.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Cosmetic animation rules during gameplay pause remain explicit; future item durations, frozen enemies, pass-through collisions, multiplayer conflict resolution, and moving-maze panning all need this central turn contract.
workBreakdown: Advance gameplay simulation only after accepted player movement or an explicit timed-mode tick.
Stop enemies, projectiles, and gameplay-affecting moving entities when the player stops unless the mode defines a timed tick.
Use one centralized simulation tick or turn command.
Define ordering for player movement, enemy movement, projectile movement, pickups, item effects, duration expiry, and collisions.
Support duration effects such as cloak enemy-freeze and pass-through collision windows.
Document inspiration at a high level without copying third-party assets or implementation.
nextActions: Draft the central turn command contract.
Decide which cosmetic animations continue while gameplay simulation is paused.
Make enemies, projectile-based items, cloak duration, multiplayer conflicts, and moving-maze panning depend on this contract.
acceptanceCriteria: Gameplay-affecting state changes only advance through the central turn command.
Entity ordering is deterministic and tested.
Paused or stopped player input stops gameplay simulation.
Cosmetic animation behavior is explicitly documented.
proofPlan: Run turn-order unit tests.
Run paused-player tests proving enemies and projectiles do not advance.
Run play-mode diagnostics proof for turn counters.

cards.title: mazer: mystery box and usable item system
cards.summary: Add mystery-box pickups that grant one held usable item with a visible action button and deterministic bounded AI evaluation.
cards.whyItMatters: Items create tactical variation, but they must be tied to enemy/combat and turn-simulation contracts before implementation.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Combat, projectile targeting, duration, probability, cloak behavior, pass-through collision, and balance rules remain unresolved.
workBreakdown: Render a one-tile mystery box with yellow question mark, blue body, animated sheen, and a three-second blue-to-light-blue pulse.
Collect the box by reaching its tile and randomly select one held item.
Show a slot-machine-style cycling animation in the bottom-right Use Item action button.
Support bullets, shield, timed speed boost, invisibility cloak, undetermined power-up, and random valid teleport as initial concepts.
Allow AI-controlled player to remember a useful nearby mystery box without always overriding its primary route.
Keep item effects deterministic enough for survival and multiplayer proof.
nextActions: Resolve item probabilities, duration, targeting, and balance.
Define how one held item replaces another.
Link cloak behavior to enemy pursuit and turn-synchronous simulation.
Link bullets and shields to enemy/combat work and movement effects to turn simulation.
acceptanceCriteria: Mystery box pickup is deterministic and bounded for tests.
The Use Item action clearly shows the selected item icon.
AI retrieval uses tactical value instead of always diverting.
Unresolved combat and balance rules are documented instead of invented.
proofPlan: Run pickup placement and item-slot tests.
Run visual proof for mystery box pulse and slot animation.
Run AI tactical-value tests once enemies exist.

cards.title: mazer: invisibility cloak item
cards.summary: Add a timed cloak item that masks the player from enemy tracking, freezes enemy movement, and lets the player pass through enemies during the effect.
cards.whyItMatters: A stealth item creates a readable emergency escape tool without changing the core maze graph or making enemies permanently harmless.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Duration, spawn source, visual language, cooldown, stacking, and multiplayer fairness rules still need design before implementation.
workBreakdown: Represent cloak duration as turn/time bounded state on the player.
Mask the cloaked player from enemy compass, tracker, pursuit targeting, and retarget scoring while active.
Freeze all enemy movement in the maze until the cloak duration completes.
Allow the cloaked player to pass through enemies without collision failure during the active window.
Show clear activation, remaining duration, and expiry feedback without exposing debug terms.
Define whether AI-controlled players can use the cloak and how enemy AI reacts after expiry.
nextActions: Choose duration units and whether duration decrements by real time, accepted turns, or game ticks.
Define pickup source: mystery box, item inventory, direct spawn, or game-mode-specific grant.
Design player/enemy visual states for cloaked, frozen, pass-through, and expiry frames.
Write collision and enemy-targeting rules before adding art.
acceptanceCriteria: Enemy tracker and compass cannot target a cloaked player during the active duration.
Enemy movement stays frozen for the whole cloak duration and resumes deterministically after expiry.
Player/enemy collision is pass-through while cloaked and returns to normal after expiry.
The cloak effect is bounded, visible to the player, and covered by deterministic tests.
proofPlan: Run turn/duration unit tests for activation, countdown, expiry, and enemy resume.
Run enemy targeting tests proving cloaked players are excluded from pursuit decisions.
Run collision tests proving pass-through only applies during the active cloak window.
Run visual proof for activation, countdown, frozen enemies, and expiry.

cards.title: mazer: multiplayer foundation
cards.summary: Add the session, identity, color, visibility, and sync contracts needed for two-player Mazer without assuming a final game mode.
cards.whyItMatters: Multiplayer changes progression, rendering, trails, collisions, and network truth; it needs a foundation before survival or moving-maze modes depend on it.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The intended local presentation is self-is-green and other-player-is-red on each device, with the other player trail hidden or always short-fading.
workBreakdown: Define multiplayer session creation, joining, identity, reconnect, and leave behavior.
Render the current user as green on their own device and the other participant as red.
Render the other participant as green on their device and this user as red there.
Hide the other player trail by default or show only a short fading trail.
Define latency, input authority, collision, item ownership, enemy targeting, and progression receipt boundaries.
Keep player-one/player-two labels local to each device instead of treating one device as globally primary.
nextActions: Choose network transport and state authority model.
Define how multiplayer receipts differ from solo and AI-runner receipts.
Design color/trail rules for accessibility and colorblind alternatives.
Decide whether multiplayer is account-only, guest-compatible, or mixed.
acceptanceCriteria: Each device sees itself as green and the other player as red.
Other-player trail is hidden or short-fading and cannot clutter route readability.
Session state survives reasonable reconnects or reports a clean leave/fail state.
Items, enemy targeting, collisions, and receipts have explicit multiplayer ownership rules.
proofPlan: Run two-client session state tests with mirrored color roles.
Run trail-visibility tests for self and other player views.
Run deterministic conflict tests for simultaneous movement, item pickup, and enemy targeting.

cards.title: mazer: endless multiplayer survival mode
cards.summary: Add an endless survival game mode where enemies and items spawn over time and players survive as long as possible, with solo and multiplayer settings.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer needs a long-form mode that uses enemies, items, and multiplayer as a survival loop instead of only endpoint completion.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The mode should support solo first if needed, then multiplayer where each device treats its own user as green and the other participant as red.
workBreakdown: Create a no-end survival objective with duration, score, wave, or survival-time tracking.
Spawn enemies over time with bounded density and difficulty scaling.
Spawn usable items over time, including future cloak and mystery-box items.
Support multiplayer role mirroring with self green and other player red on each device.
Keep other-player trail hidden or short-fading so survival readability stays clean.
Define whether players cooperate, compete, revive, collide, or share enemy pressure.
nextActions: Define survival scoring and fail conditions.
Choose spawn pacing for enemies and items by elapsed time, maze pressure, and player count.
Design HUD for survival time, wave pressure, item slot, and teammate/opponent state.
Prototype solo survival before enabling multiplayer if network foundation is not ready.
acceptanceCriteria: The mode has no endpoint clear condition and measures survival duration or score.
Enemy and item spawn rates stay bounded and readable over long sessions.
Multiplayer rendering follows local self-green/other-red rules on both devices.
The mode has deterministic tests for spawn cadence, collision/failure, item pickup, and multiplayer visibility.
proofPlan: Run survival-mode state-machine tests for spawn, scoring, and fail transitions.
Run long-session simulation tests for enemy and item density caps.
Run two-client visual proof for mirrored player colors and trail behavior.

cards.title: mazer: moving procedural maze mode
cards.summary: Add a mode where the maze pans in one or more directions while new maze space procedurally generates ahead of the player.
cards.whyItMatters: A moving maze can create pressure without a fixed endpoint, but it requires streaming topology, camera, AI, trail, and spawn rules to agree.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Direction policy, generation window, wrap behavior, enemy/item spawn ownership, and fail conditions are unresolved.
workBreakdown: Define whether the maze pans in one fixed direction, changes direction, or supports multiple simultaneous directional pressures.
Stream procedural maze cells ahead of the pan while retiring or locking cells behind the player.
Keep player movement, enemy movement, items, compass, trails, and camera aligned with the moving world frame.
Define failure when the player is pushed out, trapped, caught, or falls behind the generated safe region.
Make panning speed scale separately from maze difficulty and survival pressure.
nextActions: Design the world-window data structure before rendering the panning effect.
Decide if this is solo, multiplayer, or a variant of endless survival.
Prototype deterministic generated chunks with stable joins and no disconnected safe path.
Define enemy and item spawn rules for newly generated maze bands.
acceptanceCriteria: New maze space generates before it is needed and remains connected to the playable region.
Panning movement does not desync player, enemies, trails, items, or camera bounds.
Failure and scoring rules are explicit and deterministic.
The mode can be tested with fixed seeds and panning schedules.
proofPlan: Run chunk-generation tests for continuity across pan boundaries.
Run camera/world-frame tests proving player/enemy/item positions stay aligned.
Run visual proof for one-direction and direction-change panning schedules.
